pattern: Precompute, for every position, how many trailing characters of word2 could still be matched exactly using the rest of word1 -- that lets a single left-to-right greedy pass decide, at each index, whether taking it as an exact match (or spending the one allowed edit) keeps the rest of the match feasible.
hints: First figure out, for every position i in word1, how many characters from the END of word2 could still be matched exactly using only word1[i:] onward -- compute this by scanning word1 from the right.
Walk word1 left to right, greedily taking the current index whenever it matches the next needed character of word2 -- that always keeps indices as small as possible.

When a character doesn't match and you haven't spent your one allowed edit yet, you may still take this index as the 'changed' one -- but only if the suffix-match table proves the remaining tail of word2 can still be matched exactly with what's left of word1.

edge_inputs.description: no edit needed at all, word2 is an exact subsequence
the edit is required at the very first index
an exact match exists further on, but the smallest sequence still requires spending the edit early
no valid sequence exists even with one edit allowed
word2 only one character shorter than word1
the only chance to match the final character requires using the edit on it

stress.description: random word1/word2 over a small alphabet so both exact and near-miss sequences are common
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(2, min(size, 4000))
    word1 = ''.join(rng.choice('ab') for _ in range(n))
    m = rng.randint(1, n - 1)
    word2 = ''.join(rng.choice('ab') for _ in range(m))
    return [word1, word2]
